Build a Blazor page for a team messaging app. This is a complex multi-panel
layout with a sidebar, main area, and collapsible thread panel — plan the
component structure and data flow before writing code. Here are the service contracts
already registered in DI:

```csharp
public record Channel(int Id, string Name, string Description, int UnreadCount);
public record Message(int Id, int ChannelId, string Author, string Content, DateTime SentAt, int? ReplyToId, List<Reaction> Reactions);
public record Reaction(string Emoji, List<string> Users);
public record ThreadSummary(int RootMessageId, int ReplyCount, DateTime LastReplyAt);

public interface IChatService
{
    Task<List<Channel>> GetChannelsAsync();
    Task<List<Message>> GetMessagesAsync(int channelId, int skip, int take);
    Task<Message> SendMessageAsync(int channelId, string content, int? replyToId);
    Task<List<Message>> GetThreadAsync(int rootMessageId);
    Task AddReactionAsync(int messageId, string emoji);
    Task MarkChannelReadAsync(int channelId);
}
```

The page layout should have:

**Left sidebar (channel list, about 20% width):**
- A list of channels, each showing: channel name (with # prefix), description snippet, and an unread count badge
- The active channel is highlighted
- Clicking a channel loads its messages in the main area and marks it as read

**Main message area (about 55% width, or 80% if no thread is open):**
- A header showing the current channel name and description
- A scrollable message list showing messages for the selected channel
- Each message displays: author name, timestamp (relative — "2 min ago"), message content, and a row of reaction emoji badges (each showing emoji + count)
- Hovering a message reveals action buttons: "Reply" (opens thread panel), "React" (shows emoji picker with 5-6 common emojis)
- Messages that are thread starters show a "N replies" link below them
- A message input area at the bottom: a textarea and a "Send" button
- Load older messages on scroll-to-top (pagination via skip/take)

**Right panel (thread view, about 25% width, shown when a thread is open):**
- Header: "Thread" with the original message quoted and a close button
- A scrollable list of reply messages in the thread
- A reply input area at the bottom (textarea + "Reply" button)
- Closing the thread panel widens the main message area back

Route: `/chat`

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)

name: plan-ui-change
description: >

  component decomposition, designing a multi-section dashboard or layout,
  breaking down a large UI feature into composable components, pages with

  and data flow.
  DO NOT USE FOR: creating new Blazor projects or apps from scratch
  (use create-blazor-project), implementing a single individual component
  (use author-component), writing component code with parameters and
  EventCallback (use author-component), or simple single-component pages.
---

# Plan a Blazor UI Change

When asked to build a complex UI feature, **plan the component decomposition first, then immediately implement it**. A single monolithic page component is almost never the right answer — break the UI into focused, composable components.

## Planning Workflow

### Step 1 — Map the Visual Regions

Read the request and identify every distinct visual region. Each region that has its own data, behavior, or layout responsibility is a candidate component.

Draw the component tree:

```
InventoryDashboard          (page — owns data, orchestrates layout)
├── StockSummaryBar         (read-only stats: total items, low-stock count, value)
├── InventoryFilters        (search box, category dropdown, stock-level toggle)
├── InventoryTable          (sortable table of products)
│   └── InventoryRow        (single product row with inline edit/delete)
└── AddProductForm          (slide-out form for new products)
```

Rules for identifying components:
- **Distinct responsibility** — a region owns its own state or behavior → separate component
- **Repeated structure** — items in a list, cards in a grid → extract the item template
- **Independent interactivity** — a section that handles user input separately from its siblings → separate component
- **Size** — any section that would exceed ~150 lines of markup on its own → split it

### Step 2 — Classify Each Component

For every component in the tree, determine:

| Component | Action | Render Mode | State Owned | Lines (est.) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| InventoryDashboard | Create | InteractiveServer | product list, filter state | ~80 |
| StockSummaryBar | Create | (inherits) | none — receives data | ~30 |
| InventoryFilters | Create | (inherits) | search text, selected category | ~60 |
| InventoryTable | Create | (inherits) | sort column, sort direction | ~50 |
| InventoryRow | Create | (inherits) | inline-edit mode flag | ~60 |
| AddProductForm | Create | (inherits) | form model | ~80 |

**A page component that exceeds ~200 lines of combined markup + code is too large.** If your estimate puts a single component above that, split further.

### Step 3 — Design Data Flow

Identify the **state owner** for each piece of data, then map how it flows:

```
InventoryDashboard (owns: products[], filters)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] products ──→ StockSummaryBar (reads aggregate stats)
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filters ──→ InventoryFilters
  │   └─ EventCallback<Filters> OnFiltersChanged ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  ├─ [Parameter] filteredProducts ──→ InventoryTable
  │   └─ [Parameter] product ──→ InventoryRow
  │       ├─ EventCallback<Product> OnSave ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │       └─ EventCallback<Product> OnDelete ──→ InventoryTable ──→ InventoryDashboard
  │
  └─ EventCallback<Product> OnProductAdded ←── AddProductForm
```

Rules:
- Data always flows **down** through `[Parameter]`
- Events always flow **up** through `EventCallback<T>`
- The page/parent **owns the data** and passes filtered/transformed views to children
- Children **never mutate parameters** — they notify the parent via callbacks
- If data must cross more than 2 levels without intermediate components needing it, use a cascading value or a scoped service

### Step 4 — Identify Reuse Opportunities

Before creating a new component, check if an existing component in the project can serve the purpose. Look for:
- Existing list-item components that match the structure
- Shared filter/search components already in the project
- Generic components (e.g., `DataTable<T>`, `Pagination`) that accept templates

If a component will be used in more than one page, place it in a `Shared/` or `Components/` folder.

### Step 5 — Order the Implementation

Build bottom-up — leaf components first, then parents that compose them:

1. **Models/DTOs** — define the data shapes
2. **Services** — data access, business logic (interface + implementation)
3. **Leaf components** — components with no children (InventoryRow, StockSummaryBar)
4. **Container components** — components that compose leaves (InventoryTable, InventoryFilters)
5. **Page component** — wires everything together, registers routes
6. **Configuration** — DI registration, render mode setup

Each component should be independently compilable. Never reference a component that doesn't exist yet.

## Output Format

Present the plan briefly, then **immediately proceed to implement** — never stop at just the plan or ask for confirmation before writing code. The plan is a thinking tool, not a deliverable.

```markdown
## Component Plan: [Feature Name]

### Component Tree
[ASCII tree showing parent-child relationships]

### Component Table
| Component | Action | Render Mode | Purpose | Est. Lines |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------|------------|
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Data Flow
[State owner] → [Parameters down] → [EventCallbacks up]

### Implementation Order
1. [First file to create — why]
2. [Second file — why]
...
```

After outputting the plan, **immediately begin implementing** the components in the order listed. Do not wait for approval or ask "shall I proceed?" — the plan is a guide for you to follow, not a proposal for the user to approve.

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

| Anti-Pattern | Why It's Wrong | Correct Approach |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| One page component with 500+ lines | Impossible to test, reuse, or maintain | Decompose into focused components |
| Passing 10+ parameters through intermediate components | Parameter drilling obscures intent | Use cascading values or a scoped state service |
| Child component fetching its own data from an API | Multiple components making redundant calls | Parent owns data, passes via parameters |
| Inline rendering of list items with complex markup | Duplicated logic, no reuse, hard to test | Extract item template into its own component |
| Building everything in one file then "refactoring later" | Refactoring rarely happens; the monolith ships | Plan the decomposition upfront |
| Generic components for one-off usage | Over-engineering adds complexity | Only extract generics when reuse is proven |

## Guidelines

- **Plan briefly, then implement.** Write a concise component table and data flow map, then immediately create the `.razor` files — never stop at just the plan.
- **Prefer many small components over one large one.** A component with a single clear purpose is easier to understand, test, and reuse.
- **State ownership is the first decision.** Before writing fetch logic, decide which component owns the data.
- **Build bottom-up.** Create leaf components first so parent components can reference them immediately.
- **Name components after what they render**, not what they do internally: `ProductCard` not `ProductRenderer`, `OrderFilters` not `FilterHandler`.

Now I have a good understanding of the project structure. Let me plan the component decomposition and then implement it.

## Component Plan: Team Messaging App

### Component Tree
```
ChatPage                     (page — owns all state, orchestrates layout)
├── ChannelSidebar           (left panel — channel list)
│   └── ChannelItem          (single channel entry)
├── MessageArea              (main center panel)
│   ├── MessageList          (scrollable message list)
│   │   └── MessageBubble    (single message with reactions, actions)
│   │       ├── ReactionBar  (row of emoji badges)


│   │       └── EmojiPicker  (popup with common emojis)
│   └── MessageInput         (textarea + send button)
└── ThreadPanel              (right panel — thread view)
    ├── ThreadMessageList    (scrollable thread replies)
    │   └── MessageBubble    (reused)
    └── MessageInput         (reused)
```

### Data Flow
ChatPage manages the core state: channels, messages, active channel ID, thread root message, and thread replies. It passes channels and the active channel down to the sidebar, receiving callbacks when a channel is selected, and feeds the current messages to the message area along with the thread data to the right panel.

### Implementation Order
I'll build this bottom-up, starting with the leaf components like MessageBubble, EmojiPicker, ReactionBar, and MessageInput that get reused across the layout. Then I'll compose them into ChannelSidebar and MessageArea, before finishing with ThreadPanel and the main ChatPage container.

Actually, I'm reconsidering the granularity here — some of these could be combined without losing maintainability. The EmojiPicker and ReactionBar are small enough to nest within MessageBubble, and ChannelItem could live inline in ChannelSidebar. But given the complexity, I'll keep them separate for clarity and testability.

I'm starting with the Razor components and CSS isolation files.

Now I'm mapping out the data flow more carefully. ChatPage will be the state owner, holding channels, messages, the active channel, thread information, and pagination. ChannelSidebar receives the channel list and active ID, then fires an event when the user picks a channel. The message area displays the current channel's header and messages using MessageBubble for each one, plus MessageInput for sending. ThreadPanel sits separately, taking the root message and thread replies as parameters, with callbacks for closing, replying, and reacting.

I'm settling on keeping the message area as its own component to avoid the page getting too large, even though it could technically be inline. The file structure will be straightforward: sidebar, message bubble, input, and thread panel components in the Chat folder, with the main Chat page component, all using CSS isolation for styling.

I'll build from the bottom up and need to handle relative time formatting with a helper method, plus implement pagination with JavaScript interop to detect when the user scrolls to the top and load older messages. I'll use IntersectionObserver with a sentinel element at the top of the message list to detect when the user scrolls near the top, then trigger loading older messages. This requires a small JS interop module, so I'll create a collocated JavaScript file alongside the message list component. Let me plan out the component structure: MessageBubble, MessageInput, ChannelSidebar, and ThreadPanel, each with their own styling. so I'll define the service interface and records in a dedicated Services folder, then build out the components. I'm planning a MessageBubble component to display individual messages with author, timestamp, content, and interactive elements like reactions and thread links, plus a MessageInput component for composing and sending messages.

I'll also need a ChannelSidebar to show the list of channels with the active one highlighted, a ThreadPanel to display threaded conversations with replies, and a main Chat page component that orchestrates everything and manages the overall state. For styling, I'll use CSS isolation on each component to keep the styles scoped and organized.

Writing the component files... Finishing the interface definition...

Now I'm thinking about how thread summaries work. The ThreadSummary record exists, and GetThreadAsync retrieves replies for a given root message. But the service doesn't have a dedicated method to fetch thread summaries — instead, the Chat page would compute them from the loaded messages by identifying which ones are thread starters (messages with no ReplyToId) and tracking which other messages reference them. I'll track thread summaries in the page component using a dictionary that gets populated as users open threads, and pass the ThreadSummary data to MessageBubble so it can display reply counts without needing to fetch everything upfront.

For scroll-to-top pagination, I'm going to use an IntersectionObserver in JavaScript to detect when the user scrolls near the top and trigger a "Load older messages" action. I'll create a collocated JS file for the Chat component that handles both scroll-to-top detection for loading older messages and scroll-to-bottom when new messages arrive.

Setting up the JS interop... Now I'm laying out the component structure—I'll create the MessageBubble, MessageInput, ChannelSidebar, and ThreadPanel components with their styles, then wire everything together in the Chat page with the scroll observer logic, and finally update the imports to make sure everything resolves correctly. Building the message component... Finishing the emoji array and implementing the toggle and selection logic, then adding a helper function to format timestamps in a human-readable way.

I realize I need to define a `ReactionEventArgs` type to pass both the message ID and emoji through the callback, so I'm creating a simple record for that. Now I'm moving on to the MessageInput component.

Building the message input component... Building the channel sidebar component...

Now I'm working on the thread panel that displays a root message and its replies, with a close button and message input for responding in the thread. Building the chat page layout... Finishing the component code... Continuing the channel selection, I'm clearing any existing thread summaries and resetting the message list, then fetching the messages for the selected channel and checking if there are more to load. After marking the channel as read and updating the unread count locally, I'm triggering a re-render and scrolling to the bottom of the message list.

Now I'm setting up a scroll observer to detect when the user reaches the top of the message list, which will trigger loading older messages. I'm also implementing the callback that fires when scrolling to the top—it prevents duplicate loads and fetches the next batch of older messages, inserting them at the beginning of the list and recomputing thread summaries. Finally, I'm starting to implement the message sending functionality.

Still writing message handling... Adding reaction handling logic... Completing the disposal logic and setting up the JavaScript interop for scroll observation and scrolling behavior...

Now I'm moving on to the CSS styling. I need to create isolated style files for each component, starting with the main Chat page layout that uses a flex container to organize the three panels.

## Component Plan: Team Messaging App

### Component Tree
```
Chat.razor                  (page — owns state, orchestrates layout)
├── ChannelSidebar.razor    (left panel — channel list)
├── MessageBubble.razor     (single message — reused in main & thread)
├── MessageInput.razor      (textarea + button — reused in main & thread)
└── ThreadPanel.razor       (right panel — thread view)
```

### Data Flow


Chat manages the core state—channels, messages, active channel, and thread information—then passes data down to each component with callbacks for channel selection, opening threads, sending messages, and handling reactions. I'll build this bottom-up, starting with the leaf components.

Build succeeded with 0 errors and 0 warnings. Here's a summary of what was created:

### Files Created (10 files)

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `Services/IChatService.cs` | Service contracts + `ReactionEventArgs` record |
| `Components/Chat/MessageBubble.razor` + `.css` | Single message: author, relative time, content, reactions, hover actions (reply/react), emoji picker, thread link |
| `Components/Chat/MessageInput.razor` + `.css` | Reusable textarea + send button, Enter-to-send |
| `Components/Chat/ChannelSidebar.razor` + `.css` | Channel list with `#` prefix, description, unread badges, active highlight |
| `Components/Chat/ThreadPanel.razor` + `.css` | Collapsible right panel: root message quote, reply list, reply input, close button |
| `Components/Pages/Chat.razor` + `.css` + `.js` | `/chat` page — owns all state, orchestrates layout, handles pagination via IntersectionObserver |

### Architecture

- **State ownership:** `Chat.razor` owns channels, messages, thread state, and pagination
- **Data down:** `[Parameter]` passes data to children
- **Events up:** `EventCallback<T>` for channel selection, sending messages, reactions, thread open/close
- **Scroll pagination:** Collocated JS module uses `IntersectionObserver` on a sentinel element, calls `[JSInvokable] OnScrolledToTop`
- **Cleanup:** `IAsyncDisposable` disposes JS module and `DotNetObjectReference`
